claims.text: Counter-intelligence officer Doug Muehler, stationed at Fort Richardson Alaska in 1989, found a thumb-sized 'this area not surveyed to date' patch on a Denali National Park map roughly 60 miles west of Mount McKinley - a survey-blank in an otherwise fully surveyed state that he believes marks the location of the buried dark pyramid.

evidence.quote:
o you by PDS

Richardson just outside Anchorage Alaska

ranges rivers lakes cities old mining
towns the typical stuff but

claims.rationale: Doug Muehler is a real person with a verified DD-214; his presence at Fort Richardson in 1989 is plausible. The 'not surveyed to date' patch on a Denali map is specific but unverified - by 1989 the USGS had largely completed Alaska topographic surveying, and any remaining blanks would have mundane explanations (cloud cover, glacier shifts). The implication that the blank patch hides a buried pyramid is purely the host's speculative framing.

claims.text: In late 1992 a local Alaska news broadcast - watched by Doug and roughly 40 off-duty soldiers - reported that seismic data from a Chinese underground nuclear test had revealed a 550-foot-tall pyramid larger than the Great Pyramid of Giza buried about 100 feet below central Alaska, but the story was killed by the next broadcast and all tapes were confiscated.

claims.rationale: There is no surviving record of any such 1992 broadcast in any local Alaska news archive. The story's claim that 40 soldiers watched it is unverifiable because none have ever come forward to corroborate. The host himself notes in the verdict that the only Chinese underground nuclear test in 1992 happened in May, six months before Doug's date. The kill-the-story / confiscate-the-tapes detail is a textbook conspiracy trope with no documentary trail.

claims.rationale: The NSA is at Fort Meade and a counter-intelligence officer could plausibly have been transferred there, but the rest of the story is uncorroborated. The 'they realized they said too much' Men-in-Black gambit is a stock dramatic beat in conspiracy narratives. There's no log, no roster, no contemporaneous record.

claims.rationale: The story is told secondhand by Marty Johnson's son after Marty's death - no contemporaneous records, no other witnesses from the alleged 1959 project crew, no Bell Labs documentation. The host himself notes in the verdict 'we only have Bruce's word, same with Marty Johnson.' Bell Labs / Western Electric did do classified DoD work in the 1950s but no buried-Alaska-pyramid project appears in any declassified archive.

claims.rationale: Same epistemic problem as Marty Johnson: the story is relayed secondhand by Lee's son Bruce. No flight logs, no photographs, no other witnesses. The 'instruments fail at 5 miles, work again on the way out' detail is a frequent UFO / black-site trope. Generating power for the entire United States from a single underground installation is implausible at any reasonable energy density even with exotic physics.

claims.text: Marty Johnson's son says his father later built a small aluminum replica of the Dark Pyramid and ran a small voltage through it; the replica magnified the input enough to power Marty's entire house and farm - apparent demonstration that pyramid geometry can amplify electrical energy, consistent with theories of an ancient pyramid energy grid linking Alaska, Egypt and Indonesia.

evidence.quote:
purpose of the project was energy
distribution if they ever got this to
work we don't know but Marty Johnson's
son said his father built a replica of
the pyramid made out of aluminum he ran
a small amount of voltage through the
pyramid and that energy was then
magnified this mini pyramid produced
more than enough electricity to power
their entire house and

pyramids in Alaska Egypt Indonesia and
other places formed an ancient energy
grid they pulled electricity out of the
Z point field and transmitted it around
the planet maybe even around the solar
system free energy cover up link below
right I have an entire episode about
inventors who discovered ways to p

claims.rationale: There is no physical artifact, photograph, schematic, or independent witness to this aluminum replica. The claim that a passive geometric form magnifies electrical energy enough to power a house violates conservation of energy as understood by mainstream physics; you cannot get more energy out of a passive structure than you put in. The household-power claim alone makes this falsifiable and false at scale.

claims.rationale: Linda Moulton Howe is a real Emmy-winning UFO journalist; she has indeed been the central conduit for the dark pyramid story since 2012. The DD-214 verification is real and Doug is who he says he is. The retired-Naval-Captain corroborator is anonymous in the public record, however, which makes 'corroboration' weak; it's possible to find a sympathetic anonymous source for almost any story.

claims.text: Lee Pearson's son Bruce flew over the pyramid's alleged location in Denali and from the air spotted a roughly squared-off patch of land containing right angles, the remains of an old road absent from any map, and an unmapped airstrip - evidence of past human activity in a wilderness whose nearest mapped road is 100 miles away.

claims.rationale: Squared-off patches and apparent right angles in aerial wilderness photography are common pareidolia (the human visual system aggressively imposes geometry on noise). Many WWII / Cold War-era logging roads, abandoned mining camps, and military training sites in Alaska don't appear on civilian maps - their existence isn't evidence of anything pyramid-related. No GPS coordinates, no published photographs, no follow-up archaeological survey.

claims.text: In May 2020 Nathan Campbell hired a plane to drop him at Carey Lake in Denali National Park, planning a four-month solo trip near the dark pyramid's alleged coordinates; he stopped checking in within weeks, his gear was found at his campsite with a final diary entry 'went to get water,' and his body was never recovered - leading some to speculate he was 'deleted' for finding the pyramid.

man actually hiked to
the coordinates to see with his own eyes

plane to fly him to carry Lake in Denali
National Park just a few miles from the

location of the dark pyramid and was

the location of the dark pyramid is in
the Alaska triangle underr Ba

claims.rationale: Hikers do disappear in Denali (Christopher McCandless's 1992 death is the most famous example). Without confirming Nathan Campbell's identity in National Park Service search-and-rescue records, the story is hard to evaluate. Even if real, the obvious explanation - Alaska wilderness is genuinely dangerous, water-fetching falls and bear encounters happen - is enormously more probable than 'deleted by the pyramid's guardians.'

claims.text: The CIA's Project Stargate remote-viewing program and one of its star psychics Pat Price independently identified an underground base operated by humans and aliens working together inside Mount Hayes in the Alaska Triangle; another remote viewer recruited by Linda Moulton Howe described visiting the dark pyramid through projection and learning its true purpose - it is a charging station for UFOs, which explains the 6,500+ UFO sightings in the Alaska Triangle.

claims.rationale: Project Stargate and Pat Price are real (Stargate ran 1972-1995; Price was a celebrated remote viewer until his 1975 death). However, Stargate's declassified results were assessed in the 1995 AIR report as having no operational intelligence value. The remote-viewing-the-pyramid testimony is anonymous and unverifiable. 'Charging station for UFOs' is the host's most extraordinary claim and rests entirely on a single anonymous remote viewer's report.

claims.text: Doug Muehler's nuclear-test story does not check out: the actual Chinese underground nuclear test happened May 22, 1992, six months before Doug's late-November/early-December date; there was no Chinese test in late 1992; and Doug's story has shifted across versions, including a debunked claim that China informed the UN about the test.

d his defense the

waited 20 years to come forward maybe
some of his details are hazy he said

1992 there was no nuclear test done then
there just wasn't so there'd be no news
report of it but what's interesting is
there was a major

claims.rationale: The host's correction is factually accurate: China's 1992 underground test (Lop Nur) did happen on May 21, 1992, and was the only Chinese test that year. The host fairly notes Doug may be conflating memories rather than lying outright, but a six-month dating error in a foundational event of the story is a serious problem and a story-shift across retellings is a classic indicator of confabulation or fabrication.

dependencies.rationale: the foundational 1992 nuclear-test framing of Doug's story doesn't fit the actual Chinese test calendar

claims.text: There was a real, well-covered 5.3 magnitude earthquake in Egypt in October 1992 with at least 500 dead and weeks of UN-monitored coverage focused on the Sphinx and pyramids - the host suggests Doug may simply be conflating that pyramid-and-news memory with a Chinese-nuclear-test memory rather than lying outright.

earthquake in Egypt in
October 1992

but what what about Lee Pearson and his

same with Marty Johnson these were real
people no d

claims.rationale: The 12 October 1992 Cairo earthquake is real (Mw 5.8 actually, killed 545 people, did damage Sphinx and pyramids and triggered UNESCO/UN attention). The host's reconstruction - that a viewer 25 years later might collapse two contemporaneous TV memories (Chinese nuke earlier in 1992 + pyramid-themed Egypt earthquake coverage in October 1992) into one false 'Alaska pyramid found by Chinese nuke' memory - is a plausible cognitive-science explanation for the Doug story.

claims.text: Linda Moulton Howe is a True Believer: she does her homework but goes into stories already believing them and selectively investigates details that support the story - a common pattern among UFO and paranormal researchers - which means her endorsement of the dark pyramid is filtered through systematic confirmation bias.

evidence.quote:
Linda Molton how I followed her
work for years she was great whenever
she was on Coast to Coast with artbell
but she is a True Believer yes she does
her homework but she goes into a story
believing it and she focuses on
investigating details to support the
story lots of UFO and paranormal
researchers do this almost all of them
do I approach these stories from the
other side I go in not believing but I
don't focus on debunking I honestly try
to find as much evidence as I can to
defend a hardto believe story sometimes

100% man-made but after researching I
can tell you this for sure Almost 100%
are man-made but there are a few that
can't be explained or debunked they j

claims.rationale: This is an accurate methodological critique. Howe's body of work consistently treats fringe claims (cattle mutilations, alien base reports, etc.) as starting from a presumption of paranormal cause and seeks confirming evidence rather than running rigorous null tests. Calling this out as a confirmation-bias problem - while still respecting her work - is the right epistemic posture.

claims.text: The host's final verdict: he doesn't know if there's a pyramid buried in Alaska, but if there is, he believes it can generate energy 'just like the Great Pyramid of Giza did when the Anunnaki built it 130,000 years ago.'

evidence.quote:
ust
can't I don't know if there's a pyramid
buried in Alaska but if there is I'm
pretty sure it can generate energy just
like the Great Pyramid of Giza did when
the Anunnaki built it 130,000 years ago
prove me
wrong thank you so much for hanging out
with me today my name is AJ that's

claims.rationale: This conditional verdict has a sober first half (acknowledged uncertainty about the pyramid) and an extravagant second half (Anunnaki built the Great Pyramid 130,000 years ago for energy generation). Mainstream Egyptology dates the Great Pyramid to roughly 2560 BCE and finds no evidence of energy-generation use. The Anunnaki / 130,000 BCE framing belongs to Sitchin-derived ancient-aliens fringe and is not supported by archaeology, geology, or physics.

dependencies.rationale: depends on the unjustified premise that pyramid geometry generates exploitable energy
